title: Stochastic Processes
coreConcept: A stochastic process is a mathematical object defined as a collection of random variables, representing the evolution of a system subject to random variation over time. It is crucial for modeling sequential data and queuing systems.
theory:
## Modeling Random Evolution

When data exhibits sequential or temporal dependencies influenced by random shocks, static probability distributions are insufficient. Stochastic processes model these dynamics.

### Markov Chains
A discrete-time process exhibiting the Markov Property: the future state depends only on the current state, not on the sequence of events that preceded it (memorylessness).
$$ P(X_{t+1} = j | X_t = i, X_{t-1} = i_{t-1}, \dots) = P(X_{t+1} = j | X_t = i) $$
Dynamics are governed by a transition matrix $P$, where element $P_{ij}$ is the probability of moving from state $i$ to state $j$.

### Poisson Processes
A continuous-time counting process used to model the occurrence of random events over time or space (e.g., website traffic, server queues). The number of events $N(t)$ in an interval $t$ follows a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda t$:
$$ P(N(t) = k) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!} $$
keyTakeaways: Stochastic processes model dynamic systems that change probabilistically over time.
The Markov property simplifies complex sequences by assuming memorylessness.
Transition matrices allow for algebraic computation of long-term stationary distributions in Markov Chains.
Poisson processes mathematically model random event arrivals in continuous time.
